# Transcrição: 16-See Your Scenes

- **Fonte:** 16-See Your Scenes
- **Duração:** 04:04
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

All right, I have a trick for you. A strategy that you can use to adhere to this notion of scenes, to make sure that you're always including scenes as the structure and the framework of your story. It's comic book pains, blank comic book pages, especially if you're a visual learner, this is going to be very helpful to you. When I'm working with a storyteller and they're trying to craft their story for the first time, figure out what belongs, what doesn't, where the scenes are going to go. I'll give them a blank sheet of comic book paper like the ones here. There's lots of different kinds. It's really kind of wonderful because you can actually find comic book pains that will match the story that you're telling. If you have a very short opening scene, you can find comic book pains that have very short, small boxes to begin with, and then maybe a larger box. Some of my storytellers get really obsessed with this and will design their own comic book pains to match the story they're telling. But the beauty of the comic book pains is a couple things. First, it forces you to keep a physical location at all times. You place yourself as a stick drawing, as a circle with a name in it, into the scene, and then you draw the scene. Now, it doesn't need to have a lot of detail, and some of my storytellers just write the word. Kitchen, bathroom, they don't take time to draw the kitchen, but they identify the location and they identify the people in the scene in that location. They'll also use the comic book pains to grab onto important words or phrases, sentences that they love that they don't want to forget. They might write those into the comic book pains as well. Also, one of the trickiest things for storytellers is the transition from one scene to the next. So when you find that great transition from one comic book pain to the next, you can often write that transition in between the two pains. This can become sort of a worksheet for you to create your story, to craft your story, by deciding where are my locations, what are the order they're going to take place in, and who's going to be in each scene. It's essentially what a filmmaker does when they storyboard their movie. They write these out in great detail. They plan the shot. They plan the camera angles. We don't have to do all of that as storytellers, but what we can do is create a visual representation of the words that are going to come out of our mouths that will ultimately result in a movie in the brains of your audience. Putting it on a piece of paper, labeling locations, identifying characters and important lines can be a wonderful way for you to have a physical, tangible representation of the thing you're going to tell. One caveat. If you're not a visual person, this will likely not help you very much. I am not a visual person. I have attempted to use this comic book pain strategy. It's not very helpful to me. For me, I see the chunks of the story in my mind just as clear as day. But for many, many, many people, and I would say the majority of people, having a physical representation of it is really important.

## [03:02 - 04:04]

The other thing that this can help with enormously is remembering your story. As a teacher, I can tell you that the way we remember and learn things is through our various modalities. We remember what we hear. We remember what we see. We remember what we do and what we touch. So if you can create a visual representation of your story, as well as an audible story that you can listen to and tell, it's going to increase your chances of remembering what you have to say when it comes time to say it. So comic book pains. We provided some downloadable PDFs for you to use. You can design your own. There's actually books online filled with varieties of comic book pains if you want to go with that. Whatever method you use, create that physical manifestation of your story on ideally a single page so you can see it laid out before you. So you can begin telling stories that are guaranteed to create the movies in the minds that your audience wants.
